address: Chikun Road, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya

description.en:
## Overview

Wat Ratchaburana stands directly across the road from Wat Mahathat in the center of Ayutthaya Historical Park. While less famous than its neighbor, this temple holds a unique attraction that most Ayutthaya ruins lack: a steep, narrow staircase descending into an underground crypt inside the main prang, where visitors can see some of the oldest surviving mural paintings from the Ayutthaya period. The well-preserved central prang towers impressively over the surrounding ruins and is one of the tallest remaining structures in the park.

## History

Wat Ratchaburana was founded in 1424 by King Borommarachathirat II on the cremation site of his two elder brothers, Prince Ai and Prince Yi. The two princes had fought each other in a duel on elephant-back over the royal succession after their father, King Intharacha, died — both perished in the combat. Borommarachathirat II, the youngest brother, ascended the throne and built this temple as a memorial. In 1957, the temple made headlines when thieves broke into the main prang's crypt, leading archaeologists from the Fine Arts Department to excavate an extraordinary treasure trove of golden artifacts, votive tablets, and royal regalia — many of which are now displayed at the Chao Sam Phraya National Museum.

## What to See

The **main prang** is the centerpiece, rising dramatically above the ruins with its Khmer-influenced corn-cob shape. Climb the steep staircase on the eastern side to descend into the **underground crypt**, which consists of two narrow levels. The first level features murals with Chinese-influenced motifs of mandarins and courtly figures. The lower level contains better-preserved **15th-century frescoes** depicting episodes from the life of the Buddha, his disciples, celestial beings, and floral motifs — among the oldest mural paintings surviving from the Ayutthaya era. Outside, explore the remains of the cloisters and secondary chedis.

## Practical Info

- **Entry fee:** 50 THB for foreigners
- **Opening hours:** 08:00-18:00 daily
- **How to get there:** Central Ayutthaya island, directly north of Wat Mahathat on Chikun Road
- **Visit duration:** 30-45 minutes
- **Note:** The crypt staircase is steep and narrow — not recommended for claustrophobic visitors

best_time_to_visit: Morning when it is cooler — the underground crypt can feel stuffy in afternoon heat
tips: Visit the Chao Sam Phraya National Museum afterward to see the golden treasures excavated from this temple's crypt
The crypt staircase is steep and narrow — wear sturdy shoes and bring a small flashlight
Combine with Wat Mahathat directly across the road for a comprehensive morning of exploration
